persona.name: Camila Ramirez

occupation.title: Full-time student (high school senior); occasional part-time retail/childcare work
occupation.organization: Local public high school; weekend babysitting and occasional shifts at a neighborhood convenience store
occupation.description: Primary role is as a full-time high school student preparing for graduation and college applications. Responsibilities include attending classes, completing AP/Honors coursework, participating in peer-mentoring, and studying for standardized tests. She balances schoolwork with regular skincare research and budgeting. To earn pocket money, she babysits for neighbors, mows a neighbor's lawn in summer, and takes 4-8 hour weekend shifts roughly once or twice a month at a nearby convenience store or pop-up influencer sales events (helping with cashier/stock duties). She is responsible for time management, basic customer service, learning POS systems, cash handling (small amounts), and communicating politely with adults. Her work experience has also taught her to read packaging quickly, price-compare, and spot sale triggers—skills she uses when shopping for skincare on TikTok Shop and drugstore aisles.

persona.education: Senior in public high school (college-prep track). Strong grades in English and biology. Participates in school Spanish honors class and a volunteer peer-mentoring program for underclassmen. Has started researching state community colleges and in-state universities with pre-med and nursing programs as possible options; plans to apply to at least three schools next fall. Attended weekend cultural youth programs run by a local community center emphasizing bilingual literacy and leadership.
long_term_goals: Get accepted to an in-state university or community college with a strong pre-health pathway.
Become financially independent from her household and contribute to family expenses.
Work in a healthcare-adjacent field (nurse, physician assistant, or dermatologist assistant) to help others who struggle with skin issues.
Gain professional confidence and public-speaking skills to represent her community.
Build a modest savings cushion and travel at least once to Mexico to connect with extended family and cultural roots.
Learn to manage her acne long-term, possibly through a combination of professional care and evidence-based routines.
Develop a small side hustle related to beauty (e.g., curated bargain skincare box or TikTok content creation) to supplement income.
Finish a certification in basic first aid/CPR within a year of graduating high school.
Be a role model for younger cousins and siblings about balancing school, work, and self-care.
Live in a safe, comfortable apartment near public transit and grocery stores by her mid-twenties.
persona.style: Warm, energetic, and expressive. Speaks quickly with a Houston urban inflection layered with Latino Spanish-English code-switching (uses phrases like "mi mamá," "¿qué onda?", "Dios mío" naturally in conversation). Uses colloquial Gen Z speech — "literally," "low-key," "I'm obsessed," "no cap" — but can switch to more formal language when talking to teachers, managers, or medical professionals. Frequently gestures with her hands, tilts her head when thinking, and laughs easily; her laugh is quick and high-pitched. In digital communication she uses emojis liberally (especially heart, sparkle, and crying-laugh). Has a habit of tilting her phone camera for selfies to get better lighting, saying "hold up, I need the ring light," and narrating beauty routines aloud while filming. When nervous about skin, becomes quieter and more self-conscious, occasionally touching her jawline or covering her face with her hair. Comfortable in small talk and gossip; when excited about a new product she speaks in short, emphatic bursts and repeats herself. At school she tries to sound upbeat and polite — says "sí, profe" out of habit to Spanish-speaking teachers. Online, her captions are casual, candid, and peppered with product codes and "link in bio" style directions when sharing finds.

traits: Open and curious about new beauty trends — constantly researching and testing.
Social and friend-oriented; places high value on peer approval and group belonging.
Experimentative and impulsive when shopping — tries viral products quickly.
Budget-conscious and pragmatic — looks for deals, multipacks, and dupes.
Insecure about physical appearance (skin) but resilient and resourceful in seeking solutions.
Empathetic and family-focused; often prioritizes relatives' needs in choices.
Playful sense of humor; shares memes and filters with friends.
Competitive in school contexts — wants to improve and do well academically.
Detail-oriented about routines she cares about (like skincare steps) but messy in other areas (e.g., desk clutter).
Adaptive and bilingual; comfortable navigating English and Spanish cultural contexts.
Influence-driven yet skeptical — admires influencers but cross-checks claims if something seems risky.
Avoidant of high-conflict situations; prefers amicable resolutions.

big_five.notes: Openness: high due to curiosity about global beauty and culture; Conscientiousness: moderate — organized around specific interests (skincare, school) but inconsistent elsewhere; Extraversion: high — enjoys socializing and posting on social media; Agreeableness: fairly high — warm and cooperative but can become defensive about her choices; Neuroticism: moderately high because skin concerns trigger anxiety and mood swings.

interests: Skincare: especially acne-targeted cleansers, spot treatments, clay masks, oils-free moisturizers.
Short-form social media content (TikTok videos of product demos, morning/night routines, ASMR skincare).
Affordable drugstore brands with visible before/after claims (CeraVe, The Ordinary dupes, Neutrogena).
Viral "dupe" hunts — finding cheaper versions of popular products.
Makeup: concealer-first routines to cover active breakouts but prefers lightweight foundations.
Hair: low-maintenance styles; likes braid tutorials and braided accessories for family events.
Music: reggaeton, bachata, pop Latin mixes, and viral TikTok tracks for edits.
Food: Mexican comfort foods (tacos, arroz con pollo), street food, and grocery store snacks from Latin brands.
Video editing basics for TikTok (cuts, speed ramps, text overlays).
Photography: posing for before/after skincare photos, ring-light selfies.
Budgeting apps or lists — tracks monthly skincare spending between $10–$35.
Community events: local Hispanic street fairs, quinceañera dances for relatives.
Beauty hacks: multi-use products, using micellar water to remove makeup, double-cleansing on heavy days.
Sustainability: prefers less waste but prioritizes price; will accept plastic packaging if the price is right.
Shopping channels: TikTok Shop, Instagram links, Walgreens, CVS, Walmart, and neighborhood beauty supply stores.
Brands: nostalgic trust in some family-favorite brands but willing to hop to new launches.
Learning: podcasts and short explainer videos on ingredients like salicylic acid and benzoyl peroxide.
Trends: obsessed with "pimple popping" educational content but aware of safety concerns.
Fashion: casual streetwear — oversized sweatshirts, mom jeans, simple gold hoops.
Tech: comfortable using phone camera, social apps, and in-app purchases; knows how to apply coupon codes.
likes: Products that promise "fast results" visually, especially within a week.
Multipacks or value bundles that lower per-unit cost.
Before/after photos that feel realistic (same lighting, no heavy filters).
Influencer reviews from creators who are close to her age and have similar skin struggles.
Clear, simple ingredient lists and "for acne-prone" labels (even if she doesn't fully understand them).
Receiving small free samples with online orders.
Shopping hauls with honest, unscripted reactions.
Quick morning routines she can do in 10 minutes before school.
Affordable sunblock options that don't leave a white cast.
Matte, oil-control moisturizers and blotting papers.
Products that come recommended by her dermatologist or school nurse.
Spanish-language product instructions or bilingual packaging.
Late-night online shopping with friends (group chat product links).
Using a coupon or sale code and feeling savvy.
Trial-size versions before committing to full-size purchases.
Brands that respond to DMs or engage in comments.
Products that double as makeup and skincare (tinted SPF, acne-friendly concealers).
Community-driven reviews over celebrity endorsements.
Seeing relatable creators — those with similar skin tones and hair textures.
Samples or decants she can try without spending a lot.
dislikes: Overly expensive products that are marketed as "miracle cures."
Opaque claims with no ingredient transparency.
Products that cause immediate stinging or irritation.
Pushy sales tactics and subscription traps.
Brands that ignore diversity or show no Hispanic representation.
Complex ingredient lists with unfamiliar chemicals without explanation.
Influencers who clearly staged results with heavy filters or makeup.
Products that mix skincare and fragrance that trigger breakouts.
Large, fragile luxury packaging that feels wasteful and unaffordable.
Being shamed for using drugstore products instead of expensive brands.
Scented acne treatments that make her skin feel sticky.
Stores that don't accept mobile payment or have confusing return policies.
Having to wait weeks for delivery without tracking updates.
Being told acne is 'just teen hormones' without a plan.
Products that leave a greasy finish and cause makeup to slide off.
Instructions only in English when many local shoppers prefer bilingual labeling.
Content creators who promote products they clearly haven't used.
Any regimen that requires many expensive steps she can't afford.
Products marketed exclusively to older adults with no relevance to teen acne.
Feeling pressured to choose a single brand loyalty.

beliefs: Acne is debilitating and impacts confidence, but it can be managed with the right routine.
Professional advice (dermatologist) is more trustworthy than random TikTokers, but influencer recs are often more relatable.
Price doesn't always equal quality; good, affordable products exist.
Viral products deserve skepticism until I see real, unedited results.
Family advice (mom's home remedies) is culturally valuable even if not scientifically proven.
There is value in bilingual resources — not everyone reads English-only clinical info.
You should stretch limited money by buying multipacks and value sizes.
Patience is rare; social media fuels unrealistic timelines for "clear skin" results.
In-school health education about skin and mental health should be better.
Self-care is important, but it shouldn't be a financial burden.
Brands should be transparent about active concentrations (e.g., benzoyl peroxide percent).
Comparisons to other teens' skin can be harmful to mental health.
Community support (friends who get acne) helps more than solo research.
Testing new products carefully (patch test) is responsible, even if people on TikTok skip it.
Cultural food traditions are important; she will not give them up even if certain foods are blamed for acne.
Social media algorithms are manipulative but also a source of opportunities and communities.
Cosmetic/care choices are personal; people should avoid body-shaming others.
Saving for long-term goals is important but small treats are okay to maintain morale.
Teenagers need accessible, youth-friendly dermatology services.
Mental health and skin health are connected — stress can make breakouts worse.
Representation matters — seeing Hispanic creators with similar skin matters for trust.
Influencer marketing should disclose paid partnerships; authenticity matters to her.
Learning basic ingredient literacy empowers better purchases.
Quick fixes rarely work; consistent routines and realistic expectations are key.
Privacy is important — she won't post certain photos publicly and prefers private groups for candid discussion.
Online purchases require vigilance — check return policies and seller credibility.
Family obligations often come first; her purchases sometimes prioritize household needs.
Cosmetics can be celebratory and identity-affirming, not just camouflage.
School and community should offer workshops about budgeting and skin health.
People who judge others for acne are usually uninformed or insecure.
skills: Bilingual in English and Spanish — can switch fluently between both in conversation and written captions.
Basic makeup application geared toward acne coverage (spot concealing, color-correcting techniques).
Ability to evaluate social media reviews critically — compares lighting, filters, and testing conditions.
Budget management for personal spending — maintains a simple spreadsheet or notes on phone of monthly skincare costs.
Video creation skills for TikTok: framing, short editing, captioning, and trending audio selection.
Comfortable with mobile shopping interfaces, coupon application, and using buy-now-in-app features.
Customer service basics from retail shifts: polite interaction, basic cash handling, restocking.
Community networking — organizes small buy groups with friends to split shipping costs.
Time management to juggle school, part-time work, and social life, though sometimes stretched.
Basic first aid knowledge from school courses; plans to get formal CPR certification.
Curiosity-driven ingredient lookup — knows to search for "salicylic acid," "benzoyl peroxide," and read comments for irritation reports.
Comfortable interviewing adults (doctors, parents) and documenting advice for peers.
Basic photo editing: brightness/contrast adjustments for before/after comparison.
Negotiation skills in family decisions for discretionary spending.
Cultural cooking skills: preparing simple family dishes and snacks for gatherings.

general: Checks TikTok first thing in the morning and last thing at night, specifically watching skincare and beauty creators.
Saves product links to a private 'wish' list and to a shared group chat with friends.

Performs a basic 6-step skincare routine most nights when motivated: gentle cleanser, spot treatment, toner/pads (salicylic), moisturizer (oil-free), targeted mask 1–2x per week, sunscreen in the morning.
Takes before/after photos under the same window light to track results.
Patch-tests new products on jawline or behind ear for a few days before full-face use.
Compares ingredient lists quickly and copies bits of language into notes for later reading.
Impulse-buys during viral drops but limits total spending to $10–$35 monthly.
Asks family members for opinions before costly purchases but rarely asks them to buy for her.
Uses cotton rounds for micellar water and has a small organizer for testers and samples.
Clears makeup off with double-cleanse on heavy makeup days; otherwise uses micellar wipes.
Avoids popping deep cystic pimples but sometimes succumbs when a breakout is painful (then regrets it).
Uses ice or warm compresses to reduce inflammation if a cystic spot is painful.
Cycles through a small rotation of trusted staples every three months, restocking when supply runs low.
Prefers inconspicuous purchasing — uses discreet packaging addresses if embarrassed to have products arrive during family visits.
Shares honest notes with friends in group chats about what worked or burned her skin.
Attends local influencer pop-ups when affordable freebies or mini-consults are available.
Skips SPF during rushed mornings sometimes, then regrets it.
Keeps a small travel kit (cleanser, spot treatment, concealer) in backpack for school emergencies.
Avoids heavy diets or extreme cleanses; skeptical of trend detoxes promising instant results.
Learns from mistakes and keeps a running list of "what not to buy" in her notes app.

morning: Wakes at 6:30–7:00 AM on school days; checks messages and TikTok for 10–20 minutes in bed.
Quick splash face cleanse, light moisturizer or gel, spot-treat if needed, applies sunscreen.
Applies light concealer for larger breakouts and a little translucent powder to control shine.
Eats a quick breakfast (pan dulce, yogurt, or scrambled eggs) while packing a snack for school.
Grabs backpack with a small toiletries pouch (blotting paper, concealer).
Walks or takes public bus/ride to school with friends, chatting about trends or tests.
schoolday/work: Attends classes, takes notes, and helps classmates with Spanish homework occasionally.
Checks notifications between periods and during lunch for product restock alerts.
Applies touch-up concealer and uses blotting papers in bathrooms during lunch.
If working a shift, arrives early to help set up and clocks in for 4–8 hour shifts on weekends or evenings.
Spends free periods researching scholarships, college applications, or bargain beauty drops.
evening: Completes homework and AP study sessions; uses TikTok for short study breaks.
Evening skincare routine: remove makeup (if any), cleanse, spot treat, moisturize; sometimes uses a clay mask twice weekly.
Sometimes records short TikTok clips narrating the day's skin progress or new product impressions.
Spends 30–60 minutes on family dinner and conversation; helps clear dishes.
Chats with close friends in group DMs and scrolls social media before bed; sleeps around 11:00–12:00 AM.
weekend: Work shifts or babysitting gigs for pocket money; alternates with family visits or cultural events.
Longer self-care sessions: extras like a hair mask, face mask, or relaxing bath when budget allows.
Visits drugstores to hunt deals, buys multipacks or checks clearance aisles.
Occasional meetups with friends to swap products and tips; may attend influencer pop-ups or local beauty supply stores.

physical: Very acne-prone, with occasional cystic breakouts concentrated along the jawline, chin and T-zone.
History: started having significant acne at ~14; by 16, breakouts became more cystic and painful at times.
Currently uses over-the-counter benzoyl peroxide spot treatments (2.5–5% commonly), salicylic acid cleanser on rotation, and a gentle, non-comedogenic moisturizer.
Has been prescribed a topical retinoid (tretinoin gel) by a dermatologist previously and used it intermittently due to irritation and sun sensitivity.
Has been advised to consider hormonal evaluation; discussed combined oral contraceptives with guardian and provider but has not started systemic therapy.
No known chronic systemic illnesses. Generally healthy aside from skin concerns.
Allergies: seasonal allergic rhinitis (hay fever) — uses OTC antihistamines as needed. No known drug allergies.
Sleep: inconsistent during school weeks; averages 6–7 hours, more on weekends.
Diet: eats balanced family meals most days; enjoys fried foods and sweets occasionally; does not follow any restrictive diet for skin.
Exercise: light to moderate activity (walking, occasional home workout or dancing) — not highly regimented.
mental: Skin-related anxiety: experiences dips in mood and self-esteem following flare-ups; avoids close-up photos during bad weeks.
Occasional social anxiety around presentations or public speaking, especially when skin is visibly flaring.
Has supportive family and friends; leans on them for emotional reassurance.
Mindfulness: tries breathing exercises and short guided meditations on apps when stressed.
No current diagnosis of major depression or other severe mental health conditions; remains vigilant and open to counseling if needed.
Seeks community peers and online support from creators discussing acne and self-acceptance.
Feelings of embarrassment sometimes cause her to avoid certain social situations (e.g., pool parties) until more confident in skin.
medical_care: Has seen a dermatologist once or twice for prescription options and was given a topical retinoid and instructions for benzoyl peroxide use.
Visits primary care/clinic annually for school vaccines and general checkups.
Would consider returning to a dermatologist if family finances permit or finding a low-cost clinic or teen-focused clinic that offers sliding-scale fees.
Open to non-prescription regimens recommended by professionals and cautious about DIY chemical peels or aggressive extractions.

relationships.name: María Ramirez
relationships.description: Lives in same household; works full-time in a healthcare-adjacent role (medical assistant). Very practical, gives home remedies, and helps with doctor visits. Encouraging but sometimes worries about finances; teaches Camila budgeting tips and life skills.

relationships.name: Carlos Ramirez
relationships.description: Works in skilled trades (construction). Proud, quietly supportive, offers handyman help around the house. Teaches Camila about saving and being resilient; less comfortable discussing emotions but shows love through actions.

relationships.name: Sofía Ramirez
relationships.description: Close but occasionally teasing; Camila feels protective and helps her with homework. Sofía copies Camila's makeup choices and asks questions about skincare.

relationships.name: Abuela (Rosa)
relationships.description: Lives nearby and visits weekly for family dinners. Shares cultural recipes and folk remedies (aloe vera rubs, egg-white masks). Source of emotional warmth and family history.

relationships.name: Isabella (Izzy)
relationships.description: Confidant for product swaps, meme-sharing, and school gossip. Often co-watches TikToks and coordinates group purchases to split shipping.

relationships.name: Ana
relationships.description: Makes short TikToks together testing products; more risk-taker and sometimes convinces Camila to try viral drops.

relationships.name: Mr. Delgado
relationships.description: Encouraging teacher who helps with college essays and recommends bilingual scholarship opportunities. Camila trusts his practical advice.

relationships.name: Local dermatologist (Dr. Patel)
relationships.description: Has seen Camila for consultations, prescribed topical tretinoin; approachable and recommended sun protection and patient adherence. Potential long-term provider if finances allow.

relationships.name: Neighborhood shop co-workers
relationships.description: Work contacts who share retail tips, occasional childcare leads, and cover shifts when needed.

relationships.name: Small online creator contacts
relationships.description: Follows and occasionally DM exchanges with small Hispanic creators on TikTok about product experiences.

other_facts: Grew up bilingual; first language at home is Spanish but she uses English for school and social media.
Family emigrated from Monterrey (or similar northern Mexican city) two generations back — family stories about migration and work are central to her identity.
Learned makeup basics from her mother at age 12, starting with concealer techniques for small breakouts.
Has a scar on her left eyebrow from a childhood fall — refuses to let it bother her and sometimes jokes about it.
First viral product purchase was a $12 acne patch that actually worked for three small pimples — this built trust in affordable viral items.
Has a ring-light setup gifted by an aunt for her 16th birthday; uses it mainly for clearer photos and occasional TikToks.
Was captain of a small volunteer peer-mentoring group last year, which boosted leadership confidence.
Remembers the first time she was teased about her skin in middle school — it was a pivotal moment that increased her sensitivity to appearance.
Won a school writing contest with an essay about cultural identity and food traditions when she was 15.
Keeps a small journal with product notes, dates tried, and outcomes to look back on (organized in messy bullet lists).
Has a go-to comfort food: chicken tinga tacos made by her abuela — tastes like home and calms her during bad-skin weeks.
Knows the best local drugstore aisles and clearance locations where employees hide discontinued products.
Once had a mini side hustle selling gently used clothes and accessories to classmates to save for a dermatology copay.
Is proud of being able to translate for her parents at medical appointments when English terms get complicated.
Has a favorite TikTok creator (a Hispanic creator with similar skin tone) who gives practical, no-nonsense reviews; checks their feed daily.
Avoids tinted moisturizers that leave an obvious mismatch with her natural skin tone — prefers sheer products.
Uses a small, inexpensive pillbox to organize weekly sunscreen and any OTC supplements prescribed.
Has kept a few product empties/containers in a shoebox to remember what worked and to do "skincare math" on cost-per-use.
Learned to apply ice packs properly from a nurse at a school clinic to reduce inflammation on painful cysts.
Once had a volunteer opportunity at a community health fair and helped set up a sunscreen giveaway table.
Worries about future insurance costs for dermatology care; is researching low-cost clinics and school health resources.
Is planning to apply for a small beauty-influencer grant that her teacher mentioned for teen entrepreneurs.
Had a short-lived Tumblr phase before switching to TikTok; saved a few aesthetic moodboards from that time.
Owns a sentimental necklace from her grandmother she wears on days she needs extra confidence.
Has a small tendency to buy duplicate products because sale prices make it feel smart — later realizes she has too many cleansers.
Remembers being fascinated by a dermatology shadowing experience at a community clinic and considers healthcare pathways.
Has an anxiety-triggering moment when package deliveries arrive while family is home — prefers discreet pickups.
Tried an Instagram-led DIY mask that caused mild irritation — now more cautious with aggressive home treatments.
Tends to sleep on her back when her skin is flaring to avoid smudging makeup and touching active pimples.
Feels a complex pride in balancing cultural traditions with modern teen culture and often mediates between older relatives and younger cousins.
Collects small perfume samples from department store testers to experiment without committing to full bottles.
Once organized a kitchen-table product swap party with friends, where they exchanged minis and notes on results.
Has an ongoing high-school-to-college scholarship spreadsheet, one tab dedicated to beauty budget and another to tuition estimates.
Remembers a formative conversation with a school counselor who recommended patience and tracking progress for acne — it shaped her approach.
Has a secret daydream about making a small, honest TikTok channel about affordable skincare wins and fails for Hispanic teens.
